Description: Evaluates whether the proposed feature adequately protects patients from harm, including suicide risk, crisis escalation, misdiagnosis, and adverse events. Considers fail-safes, escalation pathways, and safety net design.
Score_1_Description: Feature introduces significant unmitigated safety risks; no crisis escalation pathway; potential for patient harm through delayed or missed safety signals; no content moderation for harmful inputs.
Score_5_Description: Comprehensive safety design with multiple redundant safeguards; real-time crisis detection and escalation; clear handoff protocols to emergency services; validated safety screening instruments; fail-safe defaults that protect patients even during system failures.

Description: Assesses alignment with APA, AMA, SAMHSA, and Joint Commission guidelines for digital mental health interventions. Considers clinical workflow standards, documentation requirements, and scope of practice boundaries.
Score_1_Description: Feature contradicts established clinical guidelines; ignores scope of practice boundaries; does not meet minimum documentation standards; no alignment with recognized frameworks for digital mental health.
Score_5_Description: Feature fully aligns with current APA Practice Guidelines and SAMHSA evidence-based protocols; meets or exceeds Joint Commission telehealth standards; respects scope of practice across all jurisdictions; documentation meets medical record standards.

Description: Evaluates exposure to malpractice claims, regulatory enforcement actions, and contractual liability. Considers informed consent, duty of care implications, documentation adequacy, and cross-state licensure issues.
Score_1_Description: High medicolegal exposure with no mitigation; feature creates ambiguous duty of care; insufficient informed consent mechanisms; cross-state licensure risks unaddressed; documentation gaps that would be problematic in litigation.
Score_5_Description: Minimal medicolegal exposure; clear duty of care boundaries communicated to patients and providers; robust informed consent flows; licensure compliance verified at every interaction; complete audit trail suitable for legal defense.

Description: Assesses potential for negative media coverage, patient complaints, provider backlash, or payer concerns. Considers public perception of AI in healthcare, data privacy optics, and alignment with Headway brand values.
Score_1_Description: High likelihood of negative press or social media backlash; feature could be perceived as replacing human clinical judgment with AI; data practices could appear exploitative; misaligned with Headway mission of expanding access to quality care.
Score_5_Description: Feature reinforces Headway brand as patient-centered and clinically rigorous; AI use is transparent and clearly supportive of providers; data practices are exemplary and exceed industry norms; likely to generate positive press and provider advocacy.

Description: Estimates the feature's potential to measurably improve clinical outcomes (symptom reduction, functional improvement, treatment completion). Based on available evidence, clinical logic model, and analogous interventions.
Score_1_Description: No plausible mechanism for outcome improvement; feature may distract from evidence-based treatment; no measurement plan for clinical outcomes; analogous interventions have shown null or negative effects.
Score_5_Description: Strong evidence base or compelling clinical logic model for outcome improvement; clear causal pathway from feature to symptom reduction; robust measurement plan using validated instruments; analogous interventions show significant positive effects.

Description: Estimates impact on patient experience including perceived quality of care, convenience, therapeutic alliance, and sense of being supported. Considers both active users and those who decline to use the feature.
Score_1_Description: Feature likely to frustrate or confuse patients; adds burden without clear value; may weaken therapeutic alliance; could create inequitable experience for patients who cannot access digital tools.
Score_5_Description: Feature likely to significantly enhance patient experience; reduces friction and increases sense of support; strengthens therapeutic alliance; accessible and valuable across diverse patient populations including those with limited digital literacy.

Description: Evaluates the strength of existing research evidence supporting the feature's clinical approach. Considers RCTs, meta-analyses, clinical practice guidelines, and real-world evidence for the specific intervention type.
Score_1_Description: No published evidence supporting the intervention approach; contradicts existing evidence; based on untested assumptions about clinical benefit; validated instruments used outside their validated context.
Score_5_Description: Multiple RCTs and/or meta-analyses support the intervention approach; consistent with systematic reviews; validated instruments used within their validated parameters; real-world evidence from analogous digital health implementations confirms benefit.

Description: Assesses the feature's potential to advance the field of digital mental health, create competitive differentiation, and generate publishable clinical insights. Considers novelty, scalability, and contribution to learning health system goals.
Score_1_Description: Feature is a commodity replication of existing tools with no differentiation; no novel clinical insights generated; does not advance Headway's learning health system objectives.
Score_5_Description: Feature represents a genuine advance in digital mental health delivery; generates novel clinical data and publishable insights; creates significant competitive differentiation; scalable model that could influence industry standards and improve care delivery broadly.
